summary: HRRR 3km CONUS convection-allowing model. The 3D pressure-level product (wrfprs) carries the full severe-env state vector but is NOT exposed by any NOMADS grib-filter CGI; only wrfsfc (2D) and wrfsubh are filterable. Full profile therefore requires byte-range subsetting against the .idx (pulls full CONUS field per message, ~36MB for a minimal profile) OR accept a thin 5-level mandatory profile from the filterable wrfsfc product. Recommend: documented TODO, byte-range path, not the RAP-style filter path.
recommendation: OPTIONAL / DOCUMENTED TODO. HRRR cleanly verifies and decodes, but it does NOT have a drop-in RAP-style grib-filter path for the dense pressure profile. Implementing the trend tool on HRRR needs real extra work (byte-range .idx subsetting + native Lambert grid nearest-neighbor + cfgrib coord-clash workarounds). RAP filter_rap.pl remains the path of least resistance for get_point_environment; HRRR is the richer-but-heavier alternative.

data_dir.idx_pattern: hrrr.t{HH}z.wrfprsf{FF}.grib2.idx   (wgrib2-style .idx present for every grib2; required for byte-range subsetting)

forecast_hours.verified: t12z wrfprs present f00-f48; t13z wrfprs present f00-f18

filter_cgi.filter_hrrr.pl: HTTP 404 (DOES NOT EXIST -- do not use; this is the obvious-but-wrong name)
filter_cgi.filter_hrrr_2d.pl: HTTP 200 -- serves ONLY hrrr.t{HH}z.wrfsfcf{FF}.grib2 (2D surface/derived product)
filter_cgi.no_filter_for_wrfprs: CONFIRMED: there is NO grib-filter CGI that exposes the wrfprs (3D isobaric) product. Spatial subregion subsetting (subregion=on&leftlon&rightlon&toplat&bottomlat) is therefore unavailable for the dense profile.
filter_cgi.note: RAP differs: filter_rap.pl exists and serves the awp130pgrb (pressure) product directly. HRRR's pressure product has no equivalent filter.

wrfprs_idx.moisture_note: wrfprs has DPT, RH, AND SPFH on pressure levels (RAP-style DPT is present; pick DPT for MetPy parcel calc). HRRR thus does NOT force you to derive Td from SPFH.

wrfsfc_filterable_profile.note: If you insist on the clean filter_hrrr_2d.pl path (spatial subregion supported), the wrfsfc product only carries a THIN mandatory-level profile -- too sparse for a high-quality trend tool.
wrfsfc_filterable_profile.severe_single_levels: same CAPE/CIN/HLCY/USTM/VSTM set as wrfprs (all present in wrfsfc too)

byte_range_subset_path.method: Per-idx-record HTTP Range GETs (bytes=start-end) concatenated into a valid multi-message grib2. NOMADS returns HTTP 206 for ranges. Multi-message concat decodes fine in cfgrib.
byte_range_subset_path.CRITICAL_no_spatial_subset: A Range GET retrieves the ENTIRE CONUS field for that GRIB message (1799x1059 = 1.9M points). There is NO way to spatially subset via byte-range. Minimal 49-record profile (7 mandatory mb levels x 5 vars + 5 single-levels + 2m DPT) = 36.6 MB. The FULL 40-level profile for 7 vars = ~225 MB. This is the heavy cost vs RAP's ~50KB filter subset.
byte_range_subset_path.records_in_fixture: TMP/DPT/UGRD/VGRD/HGT on 1000/925/850/700/500/300/250 mb + sfc CAPE/CIN + HLCY(0-1km,0-3km) + USTM/VSTM + DPT:2m

cfgrib_decode.isobaric_dims: (isobaricInhPa, y, x) -- e.g. (7, 1059, 1799) for the 7-level fixture
cfgrib_decode.GOTCHA_coord_clash: Opening typeOfLevel='heightAboveGroundLayer' raises cfgrib DatasetBuildError and SILENTLY SKIPS ustm/vstm/surface-cape: hlcy has heightAboveGroundLayer=[1000,3000] but ustm/vstm have a scalar 0.0 on the same key. You MUST read ustm/vstm/cape with a tighter filter, e.g. filter_by_keys={'shortName':'ustm'} (level resolves to scalar 0.0) and {'shortName':'cape','typeOfLevel':'surface'}. hlcy reads fine via {'typeOfLevel':'heightAboveGroundLayer'} alone.
cfgrib_decode.hlcy: filter_by_keys={'typeOfLevel':'heightAboveGroundLayer'} -> var 'hlcy' units m**2 s**-2, dim heightAboveGroundLayer=[1000.,3000.]
cfgrib_decode.d2m: 2m dewpoint decodes as var 'd2m' under typeOfLevel='heightAboveGround'

grid.GRIB_gridType: lambert (Lambert Conformal Conic) -- NOT regular lat/lon (RAP awp130 is also lambert; but coordinates here are native 3km)
grid.coordinate_arrays: latitude/longitude are 2D arrays shape (1059,1799) with dims (y,x). longitude is 0..360 (e.g. OKC lon=262.48, NOT -97.52).
grid.point_extraction: Nearest-neighbor: convert target lon to 0..360, compute argmin of (lat2d-lat)^2+(lon2d-lon)^2, isel(y=iy,x=ix). For OKC (35.47, 262.48) nearest grid pt was 0.016 deg away (3km grid).

sentinels.GRIB_missingValue: 3.4028234663852886e+38 (float32 max). cfgrib also maps undefined/bitmap-masked points to 9.999e20 in some products; treat both as missing. Mask before MetPy.

metpy_pipeline_check.steps: cfgrib decode -> 2D nearest-neighbor column extract -> attach metpy units (hPa/degC/m_s) -> sort descending pressure -> mpcalc.surface_based_cape_cin + mpcalc.bulk_shear all run without error on the OKC column.
metpy_pipeline_check.note: CAPE from the truncated 7-level fixture is only illustrative (326 J/kg); a production trend tool should fetch the dense 40-level wrfprs profile for accurate parcel ascent.

gotchas_for_implementer: filter_hrrr.pl is a 404. The HRRR filter scripts are filter_hrrr_2d.pl (wrfsfc) and filter_hrrr_sub.pl (wrfsubh) ONLY. Neither serves wrfprs.
No grib-filter exposes the dense 3D pressure product -> no spatial subregion subsetting for the full profile. This is the single biggest divergence from the RAP path.
Byte-range subsetting works (HTTP 206) but pulls full CONUS per message: ~36MB minimal, ~225MB full profile. Plan caching/timeout/size budget accordingly.

cfgrib SILENTLY SKIPS ustm/vstm/surface-cape when opening typeOfLevel='heightAboveGroundLayer' due to a coord clash with hlcy. Read those three with shortName/typeOfLevel-specific filter_by_keys or you will lose storm motion + surface CAPE without an error.

Native Lambert 3km grid with 2D lat/lon arrays and 0..360 longitudes -- you cannot use ds.sel(latitude=..., longitude=...) like a regular grid; you must do explicit 2D nearest-neighbor. RAP awp130 is a coarser 13km lambert; HRRR point extraction code is NOT reusable as-is.
Forecast hours: only the 00/06/12/18Z cycles extend to f48; all other cycles stop at f18. A trend tool that wants long lead times must pin to extended cycles.
wrfprs has DPT directly on pressure levels (good -- no SPFH->Td derivation needed), unlike some products. RH and SPFH are also present if preferred.
Use --http1.1 with curl against NOMADS (HTTP/2 negotiation can stall). httpx with http2=False works fine for the Range fetches.
The dir is real-time prod only (hrrr.{today}, ~last 2 days). For historical/archive use the AWS noaa-hrrr-bdp-pds S3 bucket (NOT verified in this probe).
